persons.arguments_against: Strongest counter-case: the surname is moderately common in the corpus (8 staff-list officials, 3 biographies named Gregg), the biography gives only initials 'B. L.' (no forename), and the early staff-list title 'Clerk to Attorney-General' is a junior post that could in principle be held by a relative. This fails because the match is not built on the junior title or initials alone: the stint records three successive and increasingly distinctive offices in Fiji - Clerk to Attorney-General (1932-37), 'Deputy Registrar of Supreme Court; Deputy Registrar-General' (1939), and 'Registrar-General and Registrar of Titles' (1953-63) - and the biography lists exactly this sequence with matching dates ('clk. to atty.-gen., 1930', 'dep. registr.-gen. and dep. registr., sup. ct., 1938', 'registr.-gen., and registr. of titles, Fiji, 1950-63'). The head-of-department posts are unique offices in a small colony; two B. L. Greggs holding the identical three-office career in Fiji over the same 30 years is not a realistic hypothesis. The biography and stint also co-occur in 9 edition-years with position agreement, and the bio's stated end date 1963 matches the stint's last listing in 1963.

evidence_cited.fact: Deputy Registrar of Supreme Court; Deputy Registrar-General
dep. registr.-gen. and dep. registr., sup. ct., 1938
registr.-gen., and registr. of titles, Fiji, 1950-63

persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second B. L. Gregg serving in Fiji's judicial/legal establishment in the same period (e.g. concurrent staff-list rows for two B. L. Greggs), or forename evidence showing the staff-list officer's initials resolve to a different person than the biography's subject.

notes: Single biography, single stint; three independent position-and-date agreements across a 31-year career in one colony, plus 9 edition co-occurrences. The 1941-49 staff-list gap is a listing gap, not a career break - the 1950 row resumes the same Deputy Registrar-General office held in 1940.
